original_abstract: While a broad consensus in policy studies suggests that Islam has exerted little to no influence on Indonesia's foreign policy, the Republic’s international relations during its revolution for independence (1945–1949) offer a compelling counter-argument. During this volatile period, the weakness of formal state structures allowed societal actors—rather than just government officials—to take a lead role in conducting diplomacy. Indonesian student groups and activists abroad successfully leveraged the concept of "Islamic Brotherhood" as a primary tool to engage the Arab world. This strategy secured early, pivotal diplomatic recognition from the Arab League and several Middle Eastern states, effectively internationalizing the struggle against Dutch colonialism. However, by 1948, the shifting landscape of the Cold War and the eruption of the Palestinian crisis refocused Arab priorities, while the maturing Indonesian state began prioritizing secular international bodies like the United Nations. Consequently, the prominence of Islam as a functional element of Indonesian foreign policy faded, marking a transition toward the non-sectarian diplomatic identity that characterizes the nation today.
humanized_abstract: Much of the literature on foreign policy in policy studies emphasizes the insignificant influence of Islam in the foreign policy of Indonesia. This article contends otherwise by referring to Indonesia’s diplomatic moves and actions undertaken during the struggle for Indonesian independence (1945-1949). Due to weak administrative conditions at that time, many societal forces carried out their foreign diplomatic activities more actively than the State Officials themselves. For instance, the student movements and Indonesian nationalist organizations had used the slogan of the ‘Islamic Brotherhood’ or ‘Ikhwan’ in order to bridge contacts with the Arab countries. Eventually they obtained the coveted diplomatic recognition for Indonesia through the mediation of the Arab League and some of the independent Arab states. International recognition of Indonesian struggle against the Dutch colonial rule on the world stage was thus in many ways made possible through the name of Islam. However, owing to the emergence of the Cold War rivalry, the unresolved crisis of Palestine in 1948, which sparked the new wave of anti-western activism in the Arab world and in other developing countries, Indonesia slowly began to change its foreign diplomatic tactics from an exclusive use of Islamic organisations and countries to more universally non-sectarian diplomatic activities particularly towards institutions such as the United Nations, hence terminating the functional aspect of Islam in Indonesian foreign policy on which ultimately contributed to the secular and pluralist diplomatic identity of Indonesia in the international political arena.
